The AI infrastructure boom is real and accelerating fast—McKinsey's $5.2T baseline capex projection by 2030 (with IT equipment and power as the big drivers) matches the explosive demand we're seeing for compute at scale. 

Betting on the "picks and shovels" like photonics, CPUs, memory, and especially power gen is the high-conviction play here. The buildout won't pause; it's the foundation for everything next. Smart call.

text:
Light Speed: Why Photonics Is the Invisible Backbone of AI

KEY HIGHLIGHT

- The Marvell $MRVL + Inphi angle is the one most people underestimate. DSP chips are to optical transceivers what the engine is to a car — you cannot ship a high-speed optical module without one, and Marvell owns that market position. Every 800G and 1.6T transceiver deployed in an AI cluster is a Marvell DSP royalty.

- Coherent $COHR is the most comprehensive pure-play but also the most complex story — the II-VI/Finisar merger created integration challenges and a leveraged balance sheet. The thesis is right but execution risk is real. Worth sizing accordingly.

- Fabrinet $FN is the sleeper that rarely gets discussed. They are the contract manufacturer for most of the photonics industry, which means they get volume leverage without technology concentration risk. When the whole industry is growing, Fabrinet grows with all of it.

- Credo $CRDO is the highest-beta, most speculative name on the list — but also the one with the most potential to surprise to the upside if the AI networking upgrade cycle accelerates faster than expected.

- And the private company to watch most closely is Ayar Labs — if optical chiplets become the standard interface for next-gen AI processors, whoever owns that technology gets acquired at a price that will seem obvious in retrospect.

I mean $SIVE is a stock I don't plan on selling.

Photonics TAM is absolutely massive for AI, robotics, consumer hardware (eg. $AAPL), Space... 

And Sivers happens to be in the bleeding edge for next-gen arhictectures and one of the few publicly traded leaders apart from $LITE and $COHR. 

TAM expansion is also massive, especially with IP acquisition downstream.

text:
Sivers Semiconductors $SIVE. The SATCOM Wildcard Nobody's Talking About 👇

Everyone's focused on Sivers as a silicon photonics / data center play. Fair. But there's a second engine quietly building underneath and it could be massive.

👉Meet ALLSPACE

They build flat-panel, multi-orbit SATCOM terminals for defense and commercial use, maritime, mobility, fixed sites. Think Starlink-grade connectivity, but hardware-agnostic across LEO, MEO and GEO.

And Sivers is their chip supplier.

Here's where it gets interesting. Each ALLSPACE terminal uses up to 1,800 Sivers chips. At up to $10,000 per terminal, the revenue math scales fast:

- 1,000 units → $10M
- 5,000 units → $50M
- 10,000 units → $100M

Volume ramp expected: H2 2026

For context, Sivers' entire 2024 revenue was ~$30M. A few thousand ALLSPACE terminals could materially move the investment case.

This is what "small volumes, big numbers" actually means in semiconductor land. You don't need mass consumer scale. You need the right customer, the right ASP, and the right timing.

text:
From our $SIVE report last night. 

Remember, we keep our reports conservative. 

Once you layer on pro‑forma net cash from the April raise, in‑place Wireless segment earnings, and legacy CST/InP photonics, total enterprise value steps up to ~SEK 4.16B (≈SEK 13.67/share).

With a further SEK 800–1,400M of unmodeled upside from IRIS², a second LiDAR win, an EU sovereign fab, and Marvell pull‑through.

Which collectively justify the convexity‑adjusted 12‑month EV range that underpins the SEK 41–45 per‑share target.

text:
It’s majority institutions moving $AXTI at these valuations, retail shorts might turn out terribly wrong.

If AXT goes down from export controls the photonics buildout does too. (Eg. AXT -> IQE -> Macom/lite) and there are bigger problems to worry about. 

If you just split out AXT’s 10+ upstream JVs into one merchant Western company it would probably be worth more strategically than 5N. 

Also revenue numbers are misleading since these are TTM contracts, not new ones signed. All the upstream materials like 7n high purity indium has spiked immensely since last year. 

It’s all vertically integrated so there might be a NAND style price hike off limited substrates, but unlike Sumitomo, AXT does the entire supply chain minus a few like red phosphorus. 

So it’s more dangerous than you think.

text:
I watched sunlight flicker on water.

The light appeared. Disappeared. Appeared again.

I thought: what if that IS a logic gate?

That question became 28 verified claims,
a complete compiler, and a new computing paradigm.

text:
$POET v $MRVL

David Lazovsky is a mechanical engineer who has over 50 patents in semiconductors and photonics. He became Executive Chairman of $POET in 2017. Before his arrival, $POET focused on GaAs. It was Lazovsky who pushed $POET to develop their optical interposer. He left $POET in 2020 and created Celestial AI because he was more keen on developing the whole photonics ecosystem. The essence is how to move data around multiple logical chips and memory, optically. Optical Multi-Die Interconnect Bridge, US Patent #12399333 and Photonic Memory Fabrics, US Patent #12271595. His development at Celestial AI was based on the optical interposer from $POET. Celestial AI signed a purchase agreement with $POET. 

$MRVL acquired Celestial AI in Feb 2026, and Lazovsky became a senior member of $MRVL. This is when the problem started, I think. $MRVL didn't spend $3.25 billion on outsourcing one of the key components in their future business. They wanted a vertically integrated system.

Lazovsky must know the gory details of $POET's optical interposer. And yet, it belongs to $POET. It looks like $MRVL decided to develop their own interposer instead of outsourcing it from $POET. The incident, breach of NDA, was just an excuse, I believe. Because the partnership between $POET and Celestial AI was known for years.

$POET lost an exceptional business opportunity. They need to find another. $MRVL and Lazovsky need to come up with a different solution to make his system work without $POET's interposer. He is a smart guy and he may have an idea already. But will it be different enough for him to be able to avoid an IP battle? $POET looks screwed, $MRVL is getting into a risky business development. But somehow I don't find this will kill $POET, because their optical interposer can work in a different setting and doesn't depend on Lazovsky's fabric. It's the other way around at the moment.

He became Executive Chairman of $POET in 2017. Before his arrival, $POET focused on GaAs. It was Lazovsky who pushed $POET to develop their optical interposer. He left $POET in 2020 and created Celestial AI because he was more keen on developing the whole photonics ecosystem. The essence is how to move data around multiple logical chips and memory, optically. Optical Multi-Die Interconnect Bridge, US Patent #12399333 and Photonic Memory Fabrics, US Patent #12271595. His development at Celestial AI was based on the optical interposer from $POET. Celestial AI signed a purchase agreement with $POET. $MRVL acquired Celestial AI in Feb 2026, and Lazovsky became a senior member of $MRVL. This is when the problem started, I think. $MRVL didn't spend $3.25 billion on outsourcing one of the key components in their future business. They wanted a vertically integrated system. Lazovsky must know the gory details of $POET's optical interposer. And yet, it belongs to $POET. It looks like $MRVL decided to develop their own interposer instead of outsourcing it from $POET. The incident, breach of NDA, was just an excuse, I believe. Because the partnership between $POET and Celestial AI was known for years. $POET lost an exceptional business opportunity. They need to find another. $MRVL and Lazovsky need to come up with a different solution to make his system work without $POET's interposer. He is a smart guy and he may have an idea already. But will it be different enough for him to be able to avoid an IP battle? $POET looks screwed, $MRVL is getting into a risky business development. But somehow I don't find this will kill $POET, because their optical interposer can work in a different setting and doesn't depend on Lazovsky's fabric. It's the other way around at the moment."}}}, "rest_id": "2048901067467862263", "source": "Twitter for iPad", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "129", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text: Seeing $TSEM going Parabolic is what got me so obsessed with $GFS doing the same thing, until I started reading how GloFo's photonics aren't exactly up to par with Towers, and realized why the stock has been so badly shorted 😵 Anyway, they are starting to catch up, and $AMD is using $GFS for the their MI500 GPUs! If I'm right, GlobalFoundries will hit $100 by early November where my Finger Icon is pointing, when it intersects the 3/1 Gann Fan Angle from it's July'22 low, and the 1/1 Gann Fan Angle from it's April'25 low.   $GFS is shorted to the gills with 40% of the float short, the photonics bonanza could definitely send this sucker to $100 in frenzy, bookmarking this post in case it turns out as predicted.

user_name: ゆるっと半導体｜AI・半導体ニュースを毎朝やさしく解説
text:
光コンピュータって聞いたことある？ボクがいま注目してるのは、オックスフォード大学発祥のLumaiという企業なんだ。

ElectronicsじゃなくPhotonics（光）でAIチップの課題を解く。通常のGPUは電子で計算するけど、光は速度が違うんだよ。消費電力も少なくて済む。

実はこのアプローチ、業界内では「次の大波」って見られてるんだ。Lumaiだけじゃなく、新興企業が光演算にシフトしてるのは、NVIDIAも監視してるぐらい。

2026年いま、データセンターの電力危機が本当に深刻化してる。そこに光が答えになるかもしれないってのが、ベンチャーから大手まで考えてることなんだ。

→ 長期投資家は、TSMC製造の半導体だけじゃなく『素材から新しい技術体系』が育つ瞬間も追う価値があるよ。

text:
Let's look at the harsh realities of scaling AI clusters and why the transition to light is mandatory:
​🔴 Copper Interconnects: Overheat and lose severe signal integrity at extreme data rates (800G and 1.6T).
🟢 Silicon Photonics: Uses lasers to transfer data near the speed of light with a fraction of the power consumption.
​With hyperscalers burning billions on electricity, is moving data optically the only way to prevent a total grid collapse?
$ASML $AVGO #semiconductors #ai
(2/4)

text:
THE PHOTONICS ROTATION

Almost nobody is watching photonics.

As AI clusters scale, copper hits physical limits and the next bottleneck becomes optical infrastructure.

Here are 15 names positioned for it:

1. $LITE owns the laser + optical switching side of the trade and is one of the cleanest pure plays on AI optical demand.

2. $COHR wins from lasers, modules, and networking hardware that power hyperscale AI infrastructure and cloud expansion.

3. $AAOI is one of the best ways to play AI optical transceivers with major hyperscaler demand for 800G and 1.6T connectivity.

4. $SIVE benefits from the push toward faster semiconductor-to-optical integration as AI infrastructure scales.

5. $MRVL controls a huge part of the DSP + interconnect story with optical networking chips and high-speed connectivity.

6. $AVGO sits at the center of AI networking through switching, custom silicon, and optical interconnect demand.

7. $ANET is the Ethernet backbone moving massive AI workloads across hyperscale clusters and data centers.

8. $GLW supplies the specialty glass + fiber needed for the optical transport layer behind AI infrastructure.

9. $JBL benefits from building and scaling the actual hardware behind networking systems and optical modules.

10. $AEHR wins from burn-in + testing demand as AI ASICs and high-power optical hardware move into production.

11. $POET is focused on lower-cost optical engines designed to improve efficiency inside AI data centers.

12. $LWLG is pushing next-gen polymer photonics that could make optical communication faster and more efficient.

13. $QCLS brings exposure to advanced laser systems supporting precision photonics and next-gen optical demand.

14. $LPTH provides specialty optics and photonic components tied to industrial, defense, and AI-driven systems.

15. $ALAB gives exposure to the connectivity + infrastructure side helping AI clusters scale faster.

Most people won’t care until these are already up 100%.

text:
Three distinct approaches to quantum hardware are now trading as the sector's most active stocks by dollar volume: trapped-ion systems with major cloud integrations, quantum annealing with hybrid solvers, and integrated photonics running at room temperature.

This lineup reveals the sheer diversity of bets the market is placing. These aren't just competitors on the same track—they represent fundamentally different architectures, each with its own thesis on how quantum advantage arrives. Cloud-accessible ion traps, purpose-built optimization machines, and portable photonic devices barely share a vocabulary, let alone a roadmap.

That diversity is a sign of a maturing ecosystem. Capital spreading across competing paradigms shows the market is pricing in genuine technical uncertainty, which is the honest position to hold right now. Nobody has a monopoly on the path to broad commercial adoption.

The common thread is that all three are building real access points for enterprise users, whether through cloud marketplaces, developer toolkits, or turnkey hardware. Providers lowering the barrier to experimentation today are positioning themselves to capture demand when quantum workloads scale tomorrow #QuantumComputing

is the most comprehensive photonics stack there is. L1: Materials & Substrates The foundation. Without this, nothing above it exists. L1 companies grow the raw crystal wafers – InP, GaAs, Ge that silicon can’t replace for high-speed photonics. Key names: $AXTI, $SOI, $IQE, $AIXA, $COHR, $LASR Top performer: AXT (AXTI) +6,837% InP demand exploded for AI datacenter optical connectivity. Q3 2025 InP revenues up 250%+ sequentially. Doubling capacity by end of 2026. L2: Active Photonic Devices This is where light gets made. Lasers, modulators, photodetectors. In a CPO world, these get embedded directly into switch ASICs – no longer pluggable add-ons. That’s a structural demand shift, not a cycle. Key names: $LITE, $COHR, $LASR, $SIVEF, $AAOI, $MTSI, $SMTC Top performer: Lumentum (LITE) +1,685% Named NVIDIA collaborator on Spectrum-X silicon photonics. Recently received the largest single CPO laser purchase commitment in company history. L3: Electro-Optical Connectivity ICs The translators. Silicon that bridges optical and electrical worlds. TIAs, drivers, DSPs, SerDes, retimers. As speeds push to 800G and 1.6T, this layer becomes a chokepoint. Key names: $MTSI, $SMTC, $MRVL, $CRDO, $ALAB, $MXLV Top performer: Credo (CRDO) +361% Not a pure photonics play – but every 800G and 1.6T deployment runs through their AEC and SerDes IP. L4: PIC Platform & IP The brain of the photonic system. Waveguides, modulators, and photodetectors on a single chip. Highest-IP layer in the stack. Whoever locks platform relationships here wins the next decade. Key names: $POET, $MRVL, $CRDO, $IOLI, $EOPT (Ayar Labs, Lightmatte) Top performer: Innolight +948% Partnered with Tower Semi on a breakthrough SiPh platform that cuts lasers per module in half. L5: Foundry & Process Platform You need a fab. Not just any fab. SiPh fabrication needs specialized processes – TFLN, SiPh COUPE, SoIC – that only a handful of foundries can run globally. Key names: $TSEM, $GFS, $UMC, $TSM Top performer: Tower Semi (TSEM) +542% SiPh revenue hit ~$52M in Q3 2025 alone, up 70% YoY. Full-year 2025 target: $220M+. Q4 annualized run rate: $320M+. Investing $650M to triple SiPh shipments by mid-2026. L6A: Engine & Module Where the optical engine becomes a product – 400G, 800G, 1.6T modules. Most commoditized layer today. CPO transition will redraw it entirely. Key names: $COHR, $LITE, $AAOI, $FN, $POET, $MRVL, $CRDO Top performer: Lumentum (LITE) +1,685% Vertically integrated across L2 and L6A. Supply locked by hyperscalers. Pricing power persists. L6B: Infrastructure Fiber and cable. The physical nervous system of every AI cluster. Boring until hyperscalers start spending $50B+ on datacenter campuses. Then it’s anything but. Key names: $GLW, $OTCPK:SMOKY, $PRY, $APH, $FJKUF Top performer: Fujikura (5803.TSE) +748% Japanese fiber giant. Direct beneficiary of AI intra-datacenter cabling demand going parabolic. L6C: Transport Moves data between datacenters across metro and long-haul fiber. As AI clusters get larger and more distributed, inter-datacenter bandwidth demand compounds. This layer doesn’t slow down. That view misses what is already happening under the surface. This is a company that has shipped tens of millions of devices and is producing at scale today. This is not a lab concept or a maybe in five years technology, it is real manufacturing with real deployment and real customers. What becomes even more interesting is where they sit in the stack. As AI infrastructure scales, the industry is running into hard physical limits, power, heat, bandwidth. One of the key architectural shifts being explored is the move toward external laser sources for optical interconnects. That is exactly where Sivers is positioned. If that approach becomes standard, they are not just another supplier, they become part of a critical bottleneck layer in next generation systems. And this is not easy technology to replicate. We are talking about sub micron manufacturing precision and extremely tight optical performance tolerances. That kind of capability is built over many years, and only a small number of companies globally can actually execute at that level. On top of that, one of the biggest risks in photonics, scaling production, has already been addressed. Through partnerships with foundries like WIN Semiconductor, there is already a path toward high volume manufacturing. That removes a major uncertainty that holds many companies in this space back. At the same time, they are quietly embedded in the global photonics ecosystem. Collaborations with research leaders like IMEC place them inside the same innovation network that feeds the broader semiconductor industry. That kind of positioning often means early access to what comes next and a role in shaping it. Put all of this together and the picture looks very different from the usual speculative small cap narrative. It starts to look more like a scaled and technically entrenched player sitting right at the intersection of where AI infrastructure is heading next."}}}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2050272539851821157"}}, "rest_id": "2050305940516204613", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "16755", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2050316023203590144", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "424", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
$LWLG Catalyst-Driven Thesis

Catalyst-driven setup accelerating in AI silicon photonics. Pre-revenue, but de-risking fast with real foundry + IP momentum. Optical interconnect bottleneck in data centers is real — low-power, high-bandwidth EO polymers targeting 200G/400G+ lanes.

Major Recent Catalysts :

• April 29, 2026: Engaged Michael Best & Friedrich LLP (Am Law 200) as strategic IP advisor. Focus: invention harvesting, patent fortress, international filings, portfolio management & licensing/tech transfer to foundries. Clear commercialization push.

• March 11, 2026: Development Agreement with Tower Semiconductor — integrating Perkinamine® polymers into PH18 SiPho platform. Multiple engineering tapeouts planned for 2026 targeting high-speed, low-power modulators.

• March 16, 2026: High-speed modulator platform now live in GDSFactory PDK for GlobalFoundries. Plug-and-play for designers & hyperscalers.
Fundamentals Snapshot (transparent):

•  Pre-revenue (~$237K in 2025) with typical development-stage losses.
•  Debt-free + solid cash runway into 2027+ via recent raise/ATM.
•  Active ATM (normal for this stage — funding R&D/tapeouts).
•  Insider sales mostly tax/option exercises post-run (standard, not dumping).
•  ~9-10% short interest, elevated but manageable. No new red flags or scandals.

Risks vs Reward: Execution on 2026 tapeouts/design wins critical. Commercial revenue targeted ~2027. High-volatility micro-cap story stock in a massive AI tailwind (optical transceivers market → $100B by 2030).

#SiliconPhotonics #AI #LWLG

text:
$SIVE $LPK $ALRIB $INFQ $LWLG 🔥
Here’s my conviction portfolio - I’ve invested the most in these five, because they sit at the epicenter of the exploding AI, photonics, quantum, and advanced manufacturing mega-trends

$SIVE – Sivers Semiconductors
Leading player in high-performance photonics lasers and mmWave wireless chips. Powering AI data centers, SATCOM, 5G/6G, and optical networks with game-changing efficiency. 

$LPK – LPKF Laser & Electronics
Precision laser systems for next-gen electronics manufacturing - PCB prototyping, laser welding, and advanced materials processing. The hidden enabler behind faster, cheaper, and more sustainable chip & component production.

$ALRIB – Riber
World leader in Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) equipment - the critical tool for growing the compound semiconductor materials used in lasers, RF chips, VCSELs, and quantum devices.

$INFQ – Infleqtion
Pure-play quantum computing building neutral-atom quantum computers, precision sensors, quantum clocks, and cold-atom tech for defense, commercial, and government customers.

$LWLG – Lightwave Logic
Pioneering electro-optic polymers (Perkinamine) that deliver ultra-high-speed, low-power photonic chips. The polymer platform that could finally solve the bandwidth bottleneck in AI data centers and silicon photonics.

Did I missed something?

text:
$QCOM is asymmetrical because it's being priced to fail.

The demand for CPUs is exploding and $ARM $INTC $AMD are the biggest winners.

But remember in February comparing $AAOI --> $LITE $COHR?  It was an underdog in photonics and then the market acknowledged it had the assets to be a credible 3rd place and deserved a higher MC.

$QCOM could be similar in that they have a credible set of assets to getting in on a different supercycle: CPUs.

This is intriguing because it trades at 12x forward PE. $ARM is 110x. $INTC is 125x. $AMD is 40x.

In 2021 Qualcomm bought Nuvia for $1.4B, the ex-Apple silicon team that designed the M-series cores which are the gold standard. At Computex 2025 they announced NVLink Fusion with Nvidia which means Qualcomm CPUs could slot into AI factories the same way Xeon does today.

On the Q3 FY25 call, Amon disclosed "advanced discussions with a leading hyperscaler" and guided data center revenue starting fiscal 2028. 

Three products are coming: a CPU, accelerators, and a full rack. The $2.4B Alphawave acquisition provides the high-speed connectivity IP to ship it.

Also in January 2025 they hired Sailesh Kottapalli, former chief architect of Intel Xeon.

Wednesday's earnings is the swing factor. Name the hyperscaler or guide data center revenue separately and there's some juice. 

At 12x forward, zero data center upside is priced in so there is clear potential for multiple expansion.

text:
NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang talked with Dwarkesh Patel that he believes that the way to grow AI is to continue to support the AI ecosystem across all facets.

"The investments that we've done with Lumentum and Coherent and all of the silicon photonics ecosystem, the last several years, we really reshaped the ecosystem and the supply chain of silicon photonics...

We're preparing the supply chain through invention of new technologies, new workflows, new testing equipment, double-sided probing, investing in companies, helping them scale up their capacity."

- CEO of @nvidia Jensen Huang on the @Dwarkesh Podcast

text:
It's highly nuanced, and I'll explain why it's not late, but late to some: 

Photonics is the newest supercycle (maybe H1 into H2 2025 was the start).

Then there's many different architectural changes in each supercycle:

-> $LITE, $COHR, Innolight, $AXTI and these names led the first 

I did a thesis post on mentioning all four of them as the largest beneficiaries (all are up 500-1000% 1Y)

-> $AAOI, $JBL and others types of names are benefit immensely as the transitional bridge (eg. 1.6T pluggable) 

-> $SIVE, Celestial, Ayar, $POET are others future gens eg. CPO (what I'm focusing on now)

-> VisEra, QD Laser, $ALMU and others are likely going to be future gens (quantum dot, different packaging types, etc) if you fast forward 4 years. 

Of course, $LITE does everything. $AXTI will be used for everything. 

But the amount of pure play exposure for each architectural shift in each mini supercycle is different. 

For example, inp usage with quantum dot is still there, but less used. Or DFB laser arrays for CPO instead of EML. 

There's probably still 50%+ with $LITE and $COHR. And you're a little on the "late" side of things. 

But you're extremely early to new architecture generations. 

What I'm trying to do is point regular retail investors into the direction of new gold mines for free. 

Before institutions figure out sooner or later by paying $20k for equity research reports.

Copper Wall Account Update 🚀

Launched this account last Monday (4/20) — already +4% in under a week.
$5/week DCA into my AI/photonics basket:

$MRVL (data center semis & networking)
$AVGO (AI networking & semiconductors)
$FN (optical component manufacturing)
$CRDO (high-speed connectivity for AI)
$TSEM (specialty analog foundry)
$ARM (CPU architecture powering AI chips)
$ALAB (AI/cloud connectivity solutions)
$TSM (world’s leading chip foundry)
$LITE (optical & photonic components)

$HON (industrial tech & automation)
$IONQ (trapped-ion quantum computing)

Thesis: Photonics could be the next major unlock in data processing, and we’re only in Year 3 of a 10-year AI buildout.

This account should look strong by 2032. 

Bullish? 💡📈

text:
THE PHOTONICS ROTATION

Almost nobody is watching photonics.

As AI clusters scale, copper hits physical limits and the next bottleneck becomes optical infrastructure.

Here are 15 names positioned for it:

1. $LITE owns the laser + optical switching side of the trade and is one of the cleanest pure plays on AI optical demand.

2. $COHR wins from lasers, modules, and networking hardware that power hyperscale AI infrastructure and cloud expansion.

3. $AAOI is one of the best ways to play AI optical transceivers with major hyperscaler demand for 800G and 1.6T connectivity.

4. $SIVE benefits from the push toward faster semiconductor-to-optical integration as AI infrastructure scales.

5. $MRVL controls a huge part of the DSP + interconnect story with optical networking chips and high-speed connectivity.

6. $AVGO sits at the center of AI networking through switching, custom silicon, and optical interconnect demand.

7. $ANET is the Ethernet backbone moving massive AI workloads across hyperscale clusters and data centers.

8. $GLW supplies the specialty glass + fiber needed for the optical transport layer behind AI infrastructure.

9. $JBL benefits from building and scaling the actual hardware behind networking systems and optical modules.

10. $AEHR wins from burn-in + testing demand as AI ASICs and high-power optical hardware move into production.

11. $POET is focused on lower-cost optical engines designed to improve efficiency inside AI data centers.

12. $LWLG is pushing next-gen polymer photonics that could make optical communication faster and more efficient.

13. $QCLS brings exposure to advanced laser systems supporting precision photonics and next-gen optical demand.

14. $LPTH provides specialty optics and photonic components tied to industrial, defense, and AI-driven systems.

15. $ALAB gives exposure to the connectivity + infrastructure side helping AI clusters scale faster.

Most people won’t care until these are already up 100%.

Marvell acquired Polariton on April 22 — a company specializing in ultra-high-performance plasmonic modulators. This likely made POET redundant on the Optical Interposer side (Marvell now has its own advanced in-house solution). BUT we must not forget the bigger picture: Marvell still desperately needs a reliable light source (lasers + External Light Sources / ELS). Polariton only provides the modulator (the “switch” that turns the light on and off). It does NOT provide the actual light itself. This is exactly where Ayar Labs + Sivers come into play. Ayar Labs is significantly more advanced than most competitors with its mature SuperNova platform — already in advanced prototyping, with proven system-level integration and strong hyperscaler interest. Ayar’s SuperNova platform uses Sivers’ high-performance multi-wavelength laser arrays as the core light engine. Why Ayar Labs is significantly better positioned than most competitors: Its SuperNova platform is one of the most mature CPO/ELS solutions on the market. Already in advanced prototyping with strong hyperscaler interest. Recently dropped Lumentum and Macom to keep Sivers as its exclusive laser supplier — a major vote of confidence. BUT competition is here : Lumentum ($LITE) and Coherent ($COHR) → already nearly fully booked through 2027-2028. Smaller players like Casela, Scintil Photonics and Xscape → solid but limited in scale and capacity. Bottom line: The POET drama is hurting short-term sentiment and creating volatility across the entire photonic AI sector. However, the core $SIVE thesis remains fully intact. Jabil’s 1.6T LRO partnership (the big industrial manufacturing arm) is completely separate and unaffected. Ayar Labs stays very well positioned, and O-Net is also active. text:
Ever since I started covering photonics...

My comments were full of "$POET, $POET, $POET"

WHY? Me nor a single photonics trader I know ever thought anything of it.

Pre rev. Paid stock promo. Now wrecked trying to hype the stock then getting $MRVL mad.

Be careful buying a stock in a hot category just because "it hasn't ran yet". That's not an investment thesis.

Here’s the landscape: $MTSI – Builds high-performance analog chips that help move data efficiently inside networks $GLW – Makes the fiber and glass that physically carries data across the world $CIEN – Provides networking gear that powers high-speed data traffic $COHR – Develops lasers and photonics components used in AI and data center systems $FN – Manufactures optical hardware that connects and scales data centers $MRVL – Designs custom chips that act as the backbone for cloud and AI infrastructure $ALAB – Focused on connectivity chips solving data flow bottlenecks in AI clusters $AVGO – Supplies critical semis across networking, storage, and AI infrastructure $AEHR – Builds testing equipment for next-gen semiconductors and photonics devices $CRDO – Creates ultra-fast interconnect chips linking AI servers at scale $AAOI – Produces optical modules that enable high-speed data transmission $LITE – Designs optical components and lasers powering AI data center connectivity This isn’t just another AI theme. It’s the plumbing behind it. And when the plumbing gets tight… that’s where the real moves start."}}}, "rest_id": "2048736457380991253", "source": "tweethunter [X]", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "11436", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2048871754857365553", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "373", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
On a day where data centers from $CIFR to $NBIS & photonics from $POET to $AAOI were pulling back strongly

Might make you think we're risk-off?

Except the rare earth/critical metals sector is greener than uranium

$USAR hit another +7% today erasing all of Friday gains as a sympathy play to $CRML's new acquisition

Can't believe it was trading for what the government paid for it last week.

woah ... my $USAR thesis is aging really well

$USAR up ~15% on the announcement to acquire Serra Verde for $2.8B in cash & stock

though the deal comes with dilution

looks like the market supports $USAR acquiring the only large-scale producer of heavy rare earths outside Asia? https://t.co/QKOGh9m1lb

1. **MRVL**: Best balance—custom AI silicon + optical interconnects, real hyperscaler revenue.
2. **TSM**: Unmatched scale as the foundry powering it all.
3. **AVGO**: Networking dominance with optical edge.
4. **ASML**: Lithography monopoly for advanced chips.

High-upside but riskier: POET/AAOI if their tech scales. AXTI/AEHR solid but more niche. DYOR, diversify.

text:
Photonics stocks ride AI's need for faster, cooler data links. All have tailwinds, but vary in moat, execution risk, and hype vs reality.

Research priority (upside + strategic edge first):

1. POET (+113% PT): Optical interposers could transform chip comms—highest reward/risk.
2. MRVL (+76%): Custom AI silicon + optics; proven hyperscaler traction.
3. AAOI (+68%): GPU transceivers; direct AI cluster play.
4. AXTI (+71%): Indium phosphide wafers; foundational material.
5. AEHR (+65%): Burn-in testing; scales reliability at volume.

Start here, then TSM/INTC/ASML as anchors. Smaller ones like LWLG/POET need deeper DD on tech viability.

Title: Utility Truck with Roof-Mounted Light Array — Photonics / LiDAR Plausibility Assessment
Timestamp: March 23, 2021 | Santa Barbara, CA (702)
Tags: #Photonics #LiDAR #UtilityVehicles #Optics #PerceptionVsReality #ForensicAnalysis
Forensic Category: Visual Assessment / Technology Plausibility

⸻

⚖️ Core Proposition

Not all high-intensity light arrays or mounted equipment indicate advanced sensing systems. However, certain configurations can overlap with technologies used in mapping, scanning, and illumination.

The task is to separate:
👉 What is commonly deployed
from
👉 What is theoretically adaptable

⸻

🔍 Observed in Image

Pickup truck with roof-mounted light bar + auxiliary lighting cluster
Trailer-mounted equipment consistent with a wood chipper / landscaping machinery
Residential driveway setting
No visibly distinct sensor housings (e.g., spinning LiDAR dome, multi-lens rig, stabilized gimbal)

⸻

🔁 Effect → Cause Analysis

Observed Effect:
Large, multi-module light array mounted above cab

Most Likely Cause (Common Use):

High-lumen LED light bars used for:
Night work visibility
Landscaping / construction operations
Off-road or utility illumination

👉 These systems can appear intense on camera due to:

Lens compression
Reflective surfaces
Sensor bloom

⸻

Secondary Consideration (Technically Plausible, but Not Evident Here):

LiDAR / photonic systems typically require:

Dedicated sensor units (often cylindrical or box modules)
Clear line-of-sight mounting
Stabilization or calibration hardware
Power + data integration (visible cabling or mounts)

👉 None of those are clearly visible in this image.

⸻

⚠️ Important Distinction

A light bar ≠ LiDAR system

However:

Some industrial vehicles can carry both lighting + sensing equipment
Advanced mapping rigs (surveying, autonomous testing) use:
LiDAR
RGB cameras
IR sensors
        —but these are visually distinct when present

⸻

🧠 Conclusion

👉 Based on visible evidence, this appears to be a standard utility/work truck with high-output lighting, not a dedicated photonic or LiDAR deployment system.

👉 While dual-use adaptation is theoretically possible in broader contexts, there is no clear visual confirmation of LiDAR or advanced sensing hardware in this instance.

⸻

📌 Provenance Note

Visual interpretation grounded in:

Known vehicle equipment configurations
Optical behavior of high-intensity LEDs
Standard LiDAR hardware characteristics

text:
$SIVE 
InP100 is a standardized platform of indium phosphide (InP) lasers and amplifier chips designed for direct integration with silicon photonics.

On‑chip light source: Enables placing the laser directly on the photonic die instead of in a separate package, reducing size, cost, and optical loss.

High single‑mode power: Delivers meaningful continuous‑wave output with narrow linewidth for better reach and signal quality.

Wafer‑scale flip‑chip readiness: Engineered for reproducible, high‑precision flip‑chip bonding to silicon or SiN waveguides.

Manufacturing friendly: Standardized epi processes and on‑wafer coatings support high yield and fast scale‑up to volume production.

Flexible form factors: Available as single emitters or compact arrays to suit different channel and power needs.

Core benefit: compact, lower‑cost, and scalable photonic systems with true on‑chip light sources, provided thermal and optical interfaces are co‑designed during integration.

text:
A lot of people still think $SIVE is just another early stage photonics story.

That view misses what is already happening under the surface. This is a company that has shipped tens of millions of devices and is producing at scale today. 

This is not a lab concept or a maybe in five years technology, it is real manufacturing with real deployment and real customers. 

What becomes even more interesting is where they sit in the stack. As AI infrastructure scales, the industry is running into hard physical limits, power, heat, bandwidth. One of the key architectural shifts being explored is the move toward external laser sources for optical interconnects. That is exactly where Sivers is positioned. If that approach becomes standard, they are not just another supplier, they become part of a critical bottleneck layer in next generation systems. 

And this is not easy technology to replicate. We are talking about sub micron manufacturing precision and extremely tight optical performance tolerances. That kind of capability is built over many years, and only a small number of companies globally can actually execute at that level. On top of that, one of the biggest risks in photonics, scaling production, has already been addressed. 

Through partnerships with foundries like WIN Semiconductor, there is already a path toward high volume manufacturing. That removes a major uncertainty that holds many companies in this space back. At the same time, they are quietly embedded in the global photonics ecosystem. Collaborations with research leaders like IMEC place them inside the same innovation network that feeds the broader semiconductor industry. 

That kind of positioning often means early access to what comes next and a role in shaping it. 

Put all of this together and the picture looks very different from the usual speculative small cap narrative. 

It starts to look more like a scaled and technically entrenched player sitting right at the intersection of where AI infrastructure is heading next.

text:
$SIVE 
The market underestimates when deep tech IP actually starts to monetize. That’s exactly what we’re seeing with Sivers Semiconductors: years of investment in laser and photonics R&D that are only now reaching a scaling phase with CPO (co-packaged optics) and AI data center demand. Retail tends to give up during the long wait, while institutions step in right when visibility improves.

The “playbook” you’re describing isn’t imaginary. With Soitec and IQE, we saw similar setups: weak sentiment, skeptical analyst coverage, followed by accumulation from large players like Morgan Stanley or Point72 before a major rerating.

text:
$COHR is stepping into a new era while the market sleeps on its potential…

You might have missed it, but Coherent recently demonstrated groundbreaking silicon photonics tech with Tower Semiconductor — a 400 Gbps modulator aimed at powering next-gen AI data centers. Plus, their strategic tie with NVIDIA is set to scale optics for advanced architectures. This isn’t just another upgrade; it’s changing how data flows.

The demand is legit. After that breakthrough in silicon carbide epitaxy capabilities, which can handle power devices up to 10 kilovolts, the stock surged by 9%. Analysts are starting to notice, and the incremental $20 billion addressable market signals that Coherent isn’t just in optical communications anymore; they’re expanding quickly into high-margin sectors.

Look, Coherent isn’t just competing in the optics space; they sit at a critical bottleneck. There are only a handful of players with real expertise in silicon photonics and high-voltage power solutions — names like Lumentum and Applied Optoelectronics can’t touch this level. Their work underpins everything the AI ecosystem needs to run efficiently, making them vital as orders pile up.

If they keep landing these partnerships, the stock won't just stabilize; it’ll be poised for a repricing as more industry shifts gear. Not many are ready for this narrative shift — people are still focused on the surface layer.

Called this before the move. If you’ve been following closely, this one should already be on your radar.

https://t.co/oVlp1VlHqQ has the rest of the watchlist. Follow + bookmark.

Todas estas compañías forman parte del superciclo de photonics: la infraestructura óptica crítica para los data centers de IA (transceivers de alta velocidad, láseres InP, silicon photonics y testing).

Nota: $AXTI publica resultados esta noche después del cierre (EPS esperado: -0,07$; Ingresos ~26,2 Mn$).

El momentum en la cadena de suministro óptica de la IA sigue intacto.

¿Qué opinas? ¿Continuará el rally o es solo rotación?

text:
$AEHR It's been interesting to watch the stock the past week, following the pullback which $POET kicked off in the silicon photonics space.

$TER reported on Tuesday. Great numbers, but the stock dropped by double digits yesterday; a drop that $AEHR didn't participate much in. Teradyne is up again today.

Is the stock ahead of itself? Yes, but it's tough to fade this rally. The acceleration is clear, and it sounds like there's a strong chance of additional orders landing in May per the CEO. How much of that is priced in? We'll find out next month.

text:
The photonics bottleneck had SO many layers.

That's what it made it so damn fun.

But I think it's messed with our heads a little on FinX.

We got a bit addicted to hunting niche small cap upstream plays because there were many.

But when it comes to insane implications of the CPU bottleneck, feels like it's more about just owning the leaders.

Tell me why $INTC $ARM $AMD can't be $1T companies when GPU:CPU ratio shifts from 8:1 to 1:1. 

Literally an 8x of the TAM on what was already a $5T buildout?

text:
$TYGO closed Fri at $5.01 (+2.45%), after-hours $5.18. Q1 earnings May 5, fresh product launches in solar/storage, analysts targeting ~$4.50-$6.

$POET exploded +28.84% to $15.10 on new orders (11-yr high), huge volume. After-hours up more. AI photonics momentum strong but volatile.

Short-term direction looks up on catalysts for both. NFA.

Sivers’ photonics division manufactures semiconductor-based optical products, including III-V compound semiconductor laser devices for LiDAR and Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) in AI data centers. They recently partnered with companies like Jabil and O-Net Technologies to supply high-performance laser arrays that help move massive amounts of data using light instead of electricity. However, they face significant competition in the optical networking and CPO space.

Written by Grok. Sivers Semiconductors AB (STO: SIVE / OTC: SIVEF) is a Swedish company specializing in advanced semiconductor technologies, with two main segments: Wireless (mmWave for 5G/6G, SATCOM, defense) and Photonics (III-V compound semiconductor lasers and arrays).0 The Photonics business is driving recent excitement, particularly in AI data centers. Core Technology for AI Sivers develops high-power Distributed Feedback (DFB) laser arrays — continuous-wave (CW) lasers that serve as light sources for high-speed optical interconnects. These are critical for moving data with light (photons) instead of electricity inside AI clusters, addressing power, heat, latency, and bandwidth bottlenecks as GPU/accelerator counts explode.28 Key advantages include: •High output power (>50mW per channel) •Wavelength stability for CWDM/DWDM •Reliability for data center environments These lasers support pluggable transceivers (e.g., 1.6T modules) and emerging co-packaged optics (CPO) / external light sources.20 gUlWK“LARGE” jUwE4“LARGE” dBwjt“LARGE” Key Partnerships & Momentum •Jabil collaboration (April 2026): Developing energy-efficient 1.6T linear receive optical (LRO) transceiver modules using Sivers lasers — targeting hyperscale AI data centers with ~2.5x lower energy footprint.20 •POET Technologies, Ayar Labs (SuperNova), O-Net, Enablence, and others for CPO/external light sources.22 •Manufacturing scale-up with WIN Semiconductors for high-volume DFB production. •Early revenue from AI/photonics customers (e.g., Ayar Labs order) plus a growing pipeline ($453M non-binding opportunities reported).45 LiDAR (autonomous/sensing) provides additional diversification and near-term revenue ramp.23 5WgcU“LARGE” Hsdff“LARGE” Why It Could Be a “Next Big Winner” in AI (High-Risk/High-Reward Thesis) AI training/inference demands ever-higher bandwidth and efficiency. Copper interconnects hit physical limits; optics (photonics) are the logical next step for intra-rack and chip-to-chip links. Sivers sits in the “picks and shovels” layer — supplying the lasers that power the optical plumbing.40 If CPO and external laser architectures gain traction (as pushed by players tied to NVIDIA/Marvell ecosystems), the addressable market for these components could be substantial. Analysts and investors model scenarios where Sivers captures a slice leading to $100M+ annual revenue by 2028 in optimistic cases, at attractive multiples for a growth story.49 The stock has already rerated sharply (hundreds of percent in the past year as of April 2026) on this narrative, now trading at a ~$900M–$1B+ market cap range depending on share price.42 Risks are real: Still pre-scale in high-volume photonics, execution on conversions from sampling to production, competition, cash burn, and dilution potential. It’s a speculative small-cap play — not guaranteed, but one of the more credible pure-plays in AI photonics. Bottom line: Sivers is a specialized enabler in the shift from electrons to photons in AI infrastructure. With real tech, ecosystem ties, and commercialization steps underway, it offers asymmetric upside if AI optics scales as expected.46 HE6AR“LARGE” EIkCL“LARGE” For the latest financials, check their investor site or recent earnings. text:
Three very different bets on the same frontier are drawing investor attention right now: trapped-ion systems delivered through major cloud platforms, quantum annealing with hybrid cloud solvers, and integrated photonics devices that operate at room temperature.

What makes this moment fascinating is the architectural diversity. The market is not converging on a single approach. It is branching out, with each pathway targeting different problem sets and deployment models.

Cloud-accessible superconducting and trapped-ion machines serve complex research and optimization workloads. Annealing systems carve out niches in logistics and materials science. Photonic approaches promise lower infrastructure overhead and novel applications in authentication and random number generation.

For businesses watching from the sidelines, the practical takeaway is that quantum is not a monolith. The technology you eventually adopt will depend on your specific use cases, latency requirements, and integration constraints, not on which stock ticker had the best week.

We are still in the infrastructure-building phase. Companies laying the groundwork today in R&D, partnerships, and IP portfolios are making long-horizon bets that commercial adoption will reward patience over hype.

Understanding what each approach actually does, and where its limitations lie, is the difference between an informed strategy and expensive guesswork #QuantumComputing

text:
The CPU bottleneck could be bigger than photonics.

Because it only gets worse as AI chips get better.

A Georgia Tech/$INTC paper makes it clear. The researchers hooked up real agentic AI workloads (research assistants, coding agents, web-search agents) and stopped to watch every step.

On a cheaper GPU setup, the CPU caused 38% of the total wait time. Then they ran the same workloads on an H200. The CPU's share jumped to 65%.

A faster GPU just means you finish the GPU computations sooner, which makes the CPU wait for all the agentic actions look even bigger by comparison.

Every accelerator upgrade only sends buyers back to order more CPUs...

This is exactly why $INTC CFO said unfulfilled demand for CPUs starts with a B.

More deep research into the CPU bottleneck in me and @KawzInvests Substack deep dive, really worked to help folks wrap their head around it in a digestible format.

$AMD $INTC $ARM $QCOM $TSM
